author: @oaffinis
text: Absolutely brilliant. Once I found a colour laser printer it only took a few minutes to go from picture to plastic. Standard PLA and cheap printer paper.  The only slightly tricky bit was getting the last bit of paper residue off the print. Must be more patient.

author: @PhilR0gers
text:
I have used this same principle to make iron-on transfers for T-shirts.  As I have a colour laser printer, I can go full colour.
I didn't think about using the heat of the molten filament to do the same onto my prints.
Thanks for that one.

Sadly I am too old, too crippled and too poor to indulge my interests any longer, so I live vicariously through the internet.  Thank you, Robert, for the content !

author: @glencahalin4786
text:
Nice.

For the glitter finish I think a bed of "cooking" parchment paper, rather than directly onto the 3d printing bed, might be a better idea. It's at the top end of it's rated use but the other side of that is, rated use is in oven for a fair amount of time. On a print bed it only has to deal with the highest temperature for a thin line at a time and then only on the, first layer, initial pass of melted filament.

For transfer use, parchment paper looks possibly also a good option, so long as it takes the laser print toner OK. I'm not sure though, as it could leave residue in the laser printer and over time start to cause problems there, like stickiness leading to paper jams...Though also not sure if it being less absorbent than normal print paper would help or hinder, I can see arguments for both cases. 

 Or indeed possibly fuse the paper to the printed filament and not be removable by soaking off like normal, water absorbent paper. So it might even be useless for either.

But full colour laser printer transfers should be possible on the type of paper you've used in the video.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am going to try the cooking paper mate - it's a nice suggestion - thanks for sharing

author: @stevennope8864
text: Could you remove the toner powder from the drum, and then "paint/dab" it on the model? Then bake in an oven to set it. It wouldn't be as sharp, but you could "paint" a full model, vs the flat surface. Also, does the toner powder mix with any solvents, like rubbing alcohol for example, that would allow an easier time "painting?" That would allow you to mix colors as needed. I'm not thinking of sharp images so much, as being able to add multiple colors to the same model without spending for a Bambu/AMS combo, and without the purge waste from one.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: this makes me think you should be able to print out a printed circuit board traces as PLA on a copper clad board and then use the bonded PLA to resist the etchant of say vinegar and hydrogen peroxide from removing the copper to leave behind a set of electrical traces for your PCB (and maybe even print standoffs and such into the board too or holders and such that if washed of the etchant when done can be left as is while soldering the board components in place ;) ) as for the printing over toner I have used clear acetate plastic (the clear sheets for using a laser printer to make over head projector slides) to laser print on then use an iron to iron the image onto everything you can think of even leather, leaving just the toner from the print on the item it was ironed onto.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I wold think you could mate

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I am still on the path to that copper nanoparticle printing by the way LOL (managed to use one of my lasers to do depositing of iron on a plastic bed using the laser on rust, and you can see the oxygen escaping so thick it looks like smoke a bit other than it dissipates too fast to be particles

replies.author: @ch5139
replies.text: @ThomasAndersonbsf I was just about to comment about acetate film, but not sure if anyone would remember overhead projectors😄, I assume it would give a super smooth first layer as well.

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @ch5139 I could see that for sure, as mine are super smooth too, even the new ones, another super smooth option would be the glossy card stock for printers and copier machines just make sure its for laser printers and you should be good (the ones for inkjet may make a gummy mess while going through a laser printer) I wish I still had access to the ricoh from back in the day AP360 I think it was) as it would print with really thick lines of color to the point it would bubble up off the paper when done. but I think we could also use the laser engravers too on low to lay down a thin layer of toner and then melt it where ever the laser touches, clean and repeat with the next color, and viola you should have a full color print to then use to 3D print on leaving it into the printed item (maybe even some white toner so you can print things that stick into and become one with a dark pigmented filament too)

If you iron the flat print thinner especially tpu you could make your own garment heat transfers for all fabric's.
Tpu hot melt powder glue is used for direct to film transfers.

author: @Forensic1Man
text: Robert, Excellent demonstration of the new transfer technique! I have an idea that could attract a lot of viewers. Consider giving a demonstration using your technique to create front panels for electronic boxes, including all the buttons, dial scales, switch positions, LED holes, and other features. Here’s the plan: (1) Use a 3D printer to make thin plates of the proper dimensions while transferring your image to a flat piece of PLA. (2) After the water treatment, simply drill all the holes and load the switches, buttons, and LEDs. (3) Attach the front panel to the box. (4) Use color prints to highlight different front panel components. This method would save a lot of time by eliminating the need to silk screen the front panels or manually draw on labels like “on/off,” “power,” and “gain.” Color Laser Jet printers can generate a very useful and beautiful front panel display that would be very cost effective!  Can you try it?

replies.text: You are right mate and I can certainly do it

author: @bartronicsecurity
text:
There are a few points here. 1. Use the laser print on inkjet gloss photo paper. It will come off a lot easier in fact with just a bit of gentle heat from a hot air gun, no water needed. 2. Not sure if you know it but there are laser printers on the market that have a straight feed through path. They can be set for up to around 1,5 mm for thick cardboard or other materials. If the plastic print is thin enough then just shove it into one of these printers.
Btw that lamp you made could be flat and the printing could be the house number or name. Put a small solar panel on it to charge that battery up during the day so it can light up at night.
